repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem
UI pickers for remote servers (like `:connect`) need to know which servers are actively used or stale. Currently, Nvim does not expose a mechanism to track user activity timestamps.

## Solution
- Introduce `v:useractive` as read-only Unix timestamp updated by `vgetc()` upon user keystrokes.
- Throttle the update to 1-sec granularity to avoid overhead.
- Expose this timestamp as `last_activity` in the dicts returned by `serverlist({info=true})` with internal RPC call.


Addresses the `[last-activity]` requirement from #39420.

**Note:** Stacked on top of #39392. Diff currently includes commits from the prerequisite PR, but will automatically shrink once #39392 merges into master.

comment_data.body: Could use `os_realtime` introduced in #39425. Pro argument is that `v:starttime` and `v:lastactivity` then have the same precision, con argument is that it complicates the 1sec debounce for precision that probably isn't needed.

Thanks for the suggestion, I've looked at #39425, but using `os_realtime()` here would actually break the throttling mechanism. It returns nanoseconds, meaning `now != last_activity` would evaluate to true on almost every single keystroke. To maintain 1-second throttle we'd have to introduce division by 1e9 in `vgetc()`, which is i think hot path.

Since the goal is just to feed a UI picker with relative time (*3 minutes ago* etc), `os_time` gives us free & dirt-cheap 1 sec throttle OOTB. I think we should stick to the cheaper integer comparision here, but that just my suggestion

`current_ui` is a similar concept, set in `input_enqueue` , any reason not to do this there also?  https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/72bc6c5801867e8f99a87737a64c1713ceac1e01/src/nvim/os/input.c#L275-L277

Hmm, good point.

`input_enqueue` is external I/O boundary, called directly from `nvim_input` (so TUI keystrokes and RPC-driven input both flow through it). `KeyTyped` in `vgetc()`, on the other hand, is only set when the key came from a real user interaction, not from mappings or macros.

If we move this to `input_enqueue`, a headless rpc plugin calling `nvim_input` would continuously update `v:lastactivity`, which might make it unreliable as a *is the human actually at the keyboard* signal. But maybe any external UI activity is exactly what we want to count here?

I can move the logic to `input_enqueue`, or just add a comment to `vgetc()` clarifying my intent. Let me know what would you prefer for this.

All keys sent by `input_enqueue()` have `KeyTyped`, so there is no change in intent.

Actually checking at `input_enqueue()` is better here. If a key typed by the user triggers a mapping, `KeyTyped` isn't set, but the user indeed has typed a key.

Thanks, then my reasoning about filtering out rpc input is no accurate. In that case, is there a meaningful difference between the two locations from your perspective, or would you prefer that I move this to `input_enqueue`?

*Edit*: I saw zeertzjq edit, thanks, that clarifies it. I'll move it to `input_enqueue()`

> If we move this to `input_enqueue`, a headless rpc plugin calling `nvim_input` would continuously update `v:lastactivity`, which might make it unreliable as a _is the human actually at the keyboard_ signal.

`nvim_input` is "human" input, by definition. That is what all UIs use.

hmm, maybe `VV_LASTACTIVITY` should not be set here, i.e. it should be `v:null` or `0`. Only actual user input should set it?

idk if it matters, but it's kind of weird that with the current impl, it's not easy to compare `v:starttime` to `v:lastactivity`. however, based on https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39423#discussion_r3153636004 it sounds like we may have to live with that.

My motivation was to avoid `nil`/null becks on the user side - pickers can always do `os.time() - v:lastactivity` without guarding. But if the intent is *last user input*, then `0` or `v:null` is more honest. Happy to change it either way

that's a good thought, but it is probably useful to be able to check that "there was no user activity since startup", right? so need some way to do that. 

if set to "epoch time", or zero, then `os.time() - v:lastactivity` still works

We could do `(now >> 30) != (last_activity >> 30)` which should still be fast and gives us a ~1.07s throttle if we do want `v:starttime` and `v:lastactivity` to be comparable.

`v:lastactivity` should then probably be initialized to `v:starttime` instead of a separate call though.

> that's a good thought, but it is probably useful to be able to check that "there was no user activity since startup", right? so need some way to do that. 
> 
> 
> 
> if set to "epoch time", or zero, then `os.time() - v:lastactivity` still works

Makes sense, I’ll initialize to `0` then.

> We could do `(now >> 30) != (last_activity >> 30)` which should still be fast and gives us a ~1.07s throttle if we do want `v:starttime` and `v:lastactivity` to be comparable.
> 
> 
> 
> `v:lastactivity` should then probably be initialized to `v:starttime` instead of a separate call though.

Bit-shift trick is clever, but I’d rather keep `input_enqueue` readable. The 1-second throttle via `os_time()` is intentional and sufficient for a UI picker. As for initializing to `v:starttime` - they intentionally use different clocks (`os_realtime` nanoseconds vs `os_time seconds`), so mixing them would require division anyway, which kind of defeats the simplicity argument.

> nanoseconds, meaning `now != last_activity` would evaluate to true on almost every single keystroke. 

I'm confused. Even if the granularity is "seconds", `now == last` is not a good way to throttle, you still should compare `now - last >= 1 second`.

> To maintain 1-second throttle we'd have to introduce division by 1e9 in `vgetc()`, which is i think hot path.

integer division is nothing compared to the rest of `vgetc`. no performance impact.

So I don't think there is any reason not to use `os_realtime`. Having different granularities for `v:lastactivity` and `v:starttime` is a UX cost with negligible benefit.
Hmm, looking at it after some time: You're right on both points. I'll switch to `os_realtime()` and use `now - last >= 1e9` for the throttle. Will update the PR in some time.
maybe `v:useractivity` expresses the semantics better?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -217,6 +217,7 @@ static struct vimvar {
   VV(VV_VIRTNUM,          "virtnum",          VAR_NUMBER, VV_RO),
   VV(VV_STARTTIME,        "starttime",        VAR_NUMBER, VV_RO),
   VV(VV_EXITREASON,       "exitreason",       VAR_STRING, VV_RO),
+  VV(VV_LASTACTIVITY,     "lastactivity",     VAR_NUMBER, VV_RO),

comment_data.body: news doesn't need to document every little detail. it's intended to surface interesting changes. the full docs are linked. thus, the abbreviated version this is overwriting is preferred.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -241,7 +241,12 @@ VIMSCRIPT
 
 • |v:exitreason| is set before |QuitPre|.
 • |v:starttime| is the process start time (nanoseconds since UNIX epoch).
-• |serverlist()| with `info=true` returns details for each server (own + peers).
+• |v:useractivity| is a nanosecond timestamp (same precision as |v:starttime|)
+  of the most recent user input (TUI or RPC nvim_input). Exposed via
+  |serverlist()| `info=true` for activity-aware pickers.
+• |serverlist()| accepts a new `info` option that returns a list of dicts
+  with `addr`, `pid`, `own` and `useractivity` for each server (own + peers),
+  enabling pickers like the future `:connect` host selector.

comment_data.body: sorry for the churn, can we name it `v:useractive`

comment_data.body: Sure thing, no problem

comment_data.body:
in this context we do not need to repeat the exact same name. here, just `active` is meaningful. also, the doc is over-explaining. since we link to `|v:useractive|` there is no need to repeat details already mentioned there.

```suggestion
		            active  (number?) |v:useractive|, or |v:null| if peer is unreachable.
```

comment_data.body: unreachable peers are already filtered out, the code only adds a peer to the list if `sockconnect` + `nvim_get_chan_info` both succeed. so the `v:null` case was effectively dead documentation. i removed it, `pid` and `active` are now documented as always-present number fields.

comment_regular_data.body: Done, I removed the initialization from `early_init`, `VAR_NUMBER` defaults to 0, which signals *no user activity since startup*.

title: feat(server): add v:useractive, use it in serverlist(info=true)
